comment_text.sn36.4:1.2: While the Buddha routinely spoke of various heavenly or lower realms, he only uses the term pātāla (“abyss”) in metaphorical senses (SN 9.1:3.3, SN 1.44:1.4). Tālapuṭa refers to it as a place, but this is in a line of poetry referring to mythical dangers of the oceans (Thag 19.1:14.2). The “Abyss” in the Purāṇas is said to be the lowest of the seven nether realms, but it is no hell. Rather, it is the glorious, jewel-festooned city of the dragons, beneath which Viṣṇu dwells, and whose glories exceed even the heavens (Śiva Purāṇa 5.15; Agni Purāṇa 120.2; Nārada Purāṇa 1.3.38). Perhaps the Buddha rejected such a notion as incompatible with his cosmology.

root_text.sn36.4:1.3: Taṁ kho panetaṁ, bhikkhave, assutavā puthujjano asantaṁ avijjamānaṁ evaṁ vācaṁ bhāsati:
root_text.sn36.4:1.5: Sārīrikānaṁ kho etaṁ, bhikkhave, dukkhānaṁ vedanānaṁ adhivacanaṁ yadidaṁ ‘pātālo’ti.
root_text.sn36.4:1.6: Assutavā, bhikkhave, puthujjano sārīrikāya dukkhāya vedanāya phuṭṭho samāno socati kilamati paridevati urattāḷiṁ kandati sammohaṁ āpajjati.
root_text.sn36.4:1.7: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ‘assutavā puthujjano pātāle na paccuṭṭhāsi, gādhañca nājjhagā’.
root_text.sn36.4:1.8: Sutavā ca kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako sārīrikāya dukkhāya vedanāya phuṭṭho samāno neva socati, na kilamati, na paridevati, na urattāḷiṁ kandati, na sammohaṁ āpajjati.
root_text.sn36.4:1.9: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ‘sutavā ariyasāvako pātāle paccuṭṭhāsi, gādhañca ajjhagā’ti.

translation_text.sn36.4:1.1: “Mendicants, when an unlearned ordinary person says that
translation_text.sn36.4:1.2: there’s an abyss under the ocean,
translation_text.sn36.4:1.3: they’re speaking of something that doesn’t exist.
translation_text.sn36.4:1.5: ‘Abyss’ is a term for painful physical feelings.
translation_text.sn36.4:1.6: When an unlearned ordinary person experiences painful physical feelings they sorrow and wail and lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion.
translation_text.sn36.4:1.7: They’re called an unlearned ordinary person who hasn’t stood up in the abyss and has found no footing.
translation_text.sn36.4:1.8: When a learned noble disciple experiences painful physical feelings they don’t sorrow or wail or lament, beating their breast and falling into confusion.
translation_text.sn36.4:1.9: They’re called a learned noble disciple who has stood up in the abyss and found a footing.
translation_text.sn36.4:2.3: that arise and sap your vitality;
translation_text.sn36.4:2.4: if you tremble at their touch,
translation_text.sn36.4:3.3: you won’t stand up in the abyss
translation_text.sn36.4:4.3: that arise and sap your vitality;
translation_text.sn36.4:4.4: if you don’t tremble at their touch—
translation_text.sn36.4:4.5: you stand up in the abyss
